one_line_takeaway: X-Mark adapts clean-label backdoor watermarking for chest-X-ray dataset ownership verification by conditioning a U-Net generator on EigenCAM saliency and adding a Laplacian-pyramid regularizer for scale-invariance, matching prior work's 100% watermark success rate while improving benign accuracy and cutting false-positive risk on independent benign models roughly 7x (p=0.058 to p=0.41) relative to its main clean-label baseline SSCL-BW.
key_finding: X-Mark achieves the highest benign accuracy of the compared methods (BA=84.88%) alongside 100% watermark success rate (WSR) on CheXpert, versus No-Attack BA of 81.89%. Among baselines: BadNets (BA 80.69%, WSR 100%, LPIPS 0.009) and Blended (BA 83.23%, WSR 97.31%, LPIPS 0.003) show high WSR but rely on detectable static triggers; WaNet (WSR 16.81%) and UBW-P (WSR 22.45%) fail to survive downsampling/multi-label settings. Clean-label baselines Label-Consistent (BA 80.69%, WSR 100%, LPIPS 0.009) and SSCL-BW (BA 82.93%, WSR 100%, LPIPS 0.023) also reach WSR 100%, but SSCL-BW produces much stronger, anatomically-unrealistic perturbations (LPIPS 0.023 vs X-Mark's 0.020) that are visible on manual inspection. For DOV (Table 2), X-Mark and SSCL-BW both achieve strong Malicious-scenario detection (X-Mark: delta-P=0.8378, p~1e-53; SSCL-BW: delta-P=0.8705, p~1e-60) and near-zero false positives in Ind-W (delta-P~-0.0005/-0.0004, p=1.0 for both). The key differentiator is Ind-M (benign-model false-positive risk): SSCL-BW yields delta-P=0.2925, p=0.058 (borderline significant, i.e., near false-positive), while X-Mark yields delta-P=0.2564, p=0.4102 -- i.e., X-Mark's p-value in Ind-M is about 7x higher than SSCL-BW's, corresponding to the paper's claimed '12% reduction in false-positive probability.' Ablations show Laplacian regularization is responsible for the BA gain (~4 percentage points, e.g. 80.24%->84.58% without/with saliency conditioning off, and 80.39%->84.88% with saliency conditioning on) and for reducing the Laplacian-pyramid loss LLap itself, while saliency conditioning alone has negligible effect on BA/WSR/LPIPS but concentrates perturbations in salient regions (qualitatively shown via EigenCAM maps in Figure 3/5). WSR reaches 100% even at very low watermarking rate gamma=1% and at a reduced perturbation budget epsilon=6/255. Scale-invariance holds down to 128x128 resolution before degrading at 64x64 and 32x32. The watermark transfers across all four tested architectures (100% WSR). Watermark survives both fine-tuning (BA/WSR stable over 100 epochs) and fine-pruning (WSR remains ~100% across all pruning rates as BA degrades), attributed to watermarked samples consistently retaining higher target-class prediction probability than benign samples.
core_contribution: X-Mark is a sample-specific, clean-label backdoor watermarking method for dataset ownership verification (DOV) of medical imaging datasets, specifically chest X-rays (CXRs). It uses a conditional residual U-Net watermark generator (with strided convolutions, reducing parameter count by 25% and MAC operations by 72% versus the vanilla U-Net used in the prior baseline SSCL-BW) with an EigenCAM-based saliency conditioning module injected at the bottleneck via convolutional projection, which steers perturbations into salient (anatomically relevant, e.g. chest) regions of each image. The generator is trained with a four-term objective: target-sample loss Lt and non-target-sample loss Lnt (both binary cross-entropy against a surrogate classifier, enforcing the clean-label dual objective for watermark embedding and verification), an LPIPS perceptual-similarity loss to preserve diagnostic quality, and a novel Laplacian pyramid loss LLap (3 pyramid levels) that penalizes high-frequency perturbation content to make watermarks robust to downsampling ('scale-invariance'), with weights lambda_t=lambda_nt=1, lambda_Lap=3, lambda_LPIPS=10, and a perturbation budget of epsilon=16/255 (L-infinity). Verification is performed via black-box hypothesis testing (one-sided paired t-test) comparing posterior probabilities of benign vs. watermarked non-target samples for the target class.
threat_model: Two parties: a dataset owner who releases a watermarked medical imaging dataset for research/legitimate use while protecting copyright, and an unauthorized user who trains a commercial model on it without permission. The owner has only black-box (API) access to a suspicious downstream model and wants to statistically verify (via probability-available hypothesis testing) whether that model was trained on the owner's watermarked data, by checking whether the model exhibits the backdoor's characteristic misclassification behavior on watermarked non-target samples versus benign samples. This is an ownership/copyright-protection threat model (defensive watermarking), not an attack paper against a victim model; the paper separately evaluates robustness of the watermark itself against two adaptive/removal attacks an unauthorized user might apply to evade detection: model fine-tuning and model pruning (fine-pruning).
limitations: The paper's own results show a genuine weakness: in the Ind-M (independent/benign-model) scenario, X-Mark's p-value of 0.4102 is not below the paper's own significance threshold of p<0.05, meaning by their own stated criterion the improvement over SSCL-BW is a reduction in false-positive risk but does not itself achieve statistical non-significance certainty in a strict sense the authors would want (they characterize it as 'a greater probability of false positives for benign models' persisting, just less than SSCL-BW). The authors explicitly attribute this to watermark perturbations being in-domain with CXR feature representations that classifiers naturally learn, causing even non-watermarked ('benign') models to exhibit backdoor-like behavior; they suggest out-of-distribution perturbation injection as a potential (unimplemented) future remedy. All experiments are single-dataset (CheXpert only) and single-modality (2D chest X-ray); the authors explicitly scope future work to extending to other medical imaging tasks (segmentation) and medical VLMs, meaning generalization beyond CXR classification is untested. The clean-label formulation and evaluation is confined to a 6-label subset of CheXpert with a single fixed target label ('No Finding'); sensitivity to different target-label choices is not reported. Perturbation budget epsilon=16/255 (L-infinity) is inherited from prior natural-image work (Li et al. 2022) rather than derived from a medical-imaging-specific imperceptibility study, though the ablation over epsilon partially addresses this. Adaptive-attack evaluation covers only two removal defenses (fine-tuning, fine-pruning); more sophisticated backdoor-detection/removal defenses (e.g., trigger reverse-engineering, spectral signatures) are not tested against X-Mark.

datasets_used: CheXpert (Irvin et al. 2019) — 224,316 frontal/lateral chest X-rays from 65,240 patients, 6 competition labels used: Atelectasis, Cardiomegaly, Consolidation, Edema, Pleural Effusion, No Finding
quantitative_results: X-Mark dataset watermarking: BA 84.88%, WSR 100%, LPIPS 0.020 (Table 1)
DOV Malicious scenario: X-Mark delta-P=0.8378, p~1e-53; SSCL-BW delta-P=0.8705, p~1e-60 (Table 2)
DOV Ind-W scenario: X-Mark delta-P=-0.0005, p=1.0; SSCL-BW delta-P=-0.0004, p=1.0 (Table 2)
DOV Ind-M scenario: X-Mark delta-P=0.2564, p=0.4102; SSCL-BW delta-P=0.2925, p=0.0580 (Table 2) — the paper's claimed '12% reduction in probability of false positives'

Ablation (Table 3): no saliency cond./no Laplacian reg.: BA 80.24%, WSR 100%, LPIPS 0.019, LLap 0.042; no cond./with LapReg: BA 84.58%, WSR 100%, LPIPS 0.019, LLap 0.014; with cond./no LapReg: BA 80.39%, WSR 100%, LPIPS 0.020, LLap 0.019; with cond.+LapReg (full X-Mark): BA 84.88%, WSR 100%, LPIPS 0.020, LLap 0.013
Watermark scale-invariance: consistent BA/WSR maintained down to 128x128 resolution; effectiveness degrades at 64x64 and 32x32
Model-agnostic transferability: 100% WSR across ResNet18, ResNet34, VGG16-BN, VGG19-BN
WSR reaches 100% even at watermarking rate gamma=1% (BA unaffected); very low gamma=0.01% also shows elevated WSR attributed to benign-model sensitivity
WSR reaches 100% at perturbation budget as low as epsilon=6/255
Fine-tuning defense: BA and WSR remain stable through 100 fine-tuning epochs on 10% of benign data
Fine-pruning defense: WSR remains ~100% across pruning rates beta in {0,...,0.98} while BA gradually decreases
Architecture efficiency: residual U-Net with strided convolutions reduces parameter count by 25% and MAC operations by 72% compared to the original U-Net used by SSCL-BW
baselines_compared: SSCL-BW (Wang et al. 2025, clean-label — primary baseline that X-Mark directly builds on and modifies)
